result:
I’ve been thinking lately about the way light behaves when no one is watching. Not the physics of it—photons and wavelengths and all that—but the mood of it. How it waits. How it practices small kindnesses on the undersides of leaves, on the quiet face of a parked car, on the wall just above the baseboard where the paint is still a little soft from last year’s brush. It keeps its own counsel, never announcing “I’m here,” yet everything it touches begins to change: the temperature of a color, the courage of a seed, the memory inside a photograph that hasn’t been opened in decades.

We talk about “capturing” light in photography, as if it were a moth we could cup in our palms. But light isn’t catchable; it’s the one doing the catching. It nets us. It takes us hostage in retrospect, years later, when we notice that the picture of our grandmother on the porch steps isn’t really about her— it’s about the way the late-July light decided to settle on her cheekbones just so, holding her face together long enough for the shutter to blink. Now she is gone, but that decision of light remains, an emissary from a single afternoon that refuses to die.

Sometimes I stand at the window at 4:17 p.m.—the hour when the sun remembers it has errands to run—and I feel the day tilt. A secret pivot. The light leans in close, lowers its voice, says: Notice. I do. I notice the neighbor’s kid dragging a stick along the fence, leaving a faint trail of sound that no one will ever replay. I notice the sparrow bathing in a pothole puddle, water flicking off its wings like tiny coins tossed into the air. I notice my own reflection in the glass, superimposed on the world outside, a ghost who pays rent to occupy his own life.

There is a dare implicit in daylight: before I go, give me form. So we do. We rake leaves into piles, we stack bricks, we arrange our faces into expressions that seem durable. We write letters, paint walls, plant tulips too deep so squirrels won’t spy the bulbs. All of it an attempt to bargain with the departing day: take this, remember me. Light accepts our offerings like a traveler who pockets small change from foreign countries—polite, amused, already halfway to the next border.

And yet, for all its travel, light is never late. It arrives exactly when it should, crossing however many million silent miles to fall, at last, on the kitchen table where a quart of strawberries glows like a still-life that hasn’t realized it’s art. One berry, split open, shows its inner cathedral: seeds in orderly pews, crimson vaulting. A moment of edible architecture. I lift it, taste it, and for an instant the summer that invented it is inside my mouth—sun, rain, the slow unbecoming of a blossom into sweetness. Then it’s gone, translated into the simple fact of being alive, which is itself a kind of light we carry under the tongue and cannot adequately explain to anyone.

Night will come, of course. It always does, sliding the horizon shut like the side of a Zippo case. But even darkness isn’t light’s opposite; it’s its archive. The stars are just old headlines that light never bothered to stop delivering. Somewhere, the light that left your body last year is still traveling, a thread pulling outward, stitching you into a fabric enormous enough to include every glance you ever gave or withheld. And because the universe has no wall, that thread will never snag. It simply goes, taking its portion of you with it, a small caravan of fingerprints and almost-remembered dreams.

So I don’t worry about being remembered in the way monuments are remembered. I’d rather be the hour nobody photographed, the one in which the light chose the bowl of oranges, the crack in the sidewalk, the top of your left ear, and said: stay. If I can be anything once I’m no longer here, let me be that gratuitous shimmer, the kind that asks for nothing but your briefest recognition, the quiet oh inside you when you realize the day has been holding your face gently in its hands all this time, and now—now it lets go.
